tip_title: Not All Water Damage Is Treated the Same on Your Home Policy
topic: Water Damage Home Insurance - Sudden vs. Gradual and Neglect Exclusions
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Not All Water Damage Is Treated the Same on Your Home Policy**

Here's something that surprises a lot of homeowners when they file a claim: standard home insurance covers sudden, accidental water damage — a pipe that bursts, a washing machine that fails — but it does NOT cover damage from neglect or gradual leaks. That slow drip under the sink that's been going for six months? The insurer is likely to say you should have caught that and deny the claim. The distinction matters because it shifts real responsibility to the homeowner to catch problems early, not just assume the policy is a safety net for anything wet.

If you're not sure how your policy draws that line or what your claim process looks like for water damage, your Peachy agent can walk you through it.

📖 [Learn more from Policygenius](https://www.policygenius.com/homeowners-insurance/does-homeowners-insurance-cover-water-damage-from-rain/)
summary: Covers the sudden-vs-gradual water damage exclusion in standard homeowners policies — specifically that neglect or slow leaks are typically denied, while sudden accidental damage is covered — distinct from prior flood-vs-rain framing.
scoring_notes: Prior water damage tips (April 22 and June 14) focused on the flood-vs-rain coverage split and the need for separate flood coverage. This tip takes a substantively different angle: the sudden-vs-gradual exclusion and how neglect voids coverage. Relevant for all three newsletters given high homeownership rates (78% East Cobb, 55% Perimeter, 65% Lewisville Lake). Timeliness is solid — heavy summer rains in Georgia and North Texas make water damage top of mind. Trusted source (policygenius.com). Angle explicitly differentiated from prior tips in scoring notes.

tip_title: Collision Covers the Car You Hit. Comprehensive Covers Everything Else.
topic: Comprehensive vs Collision Auto Coverage - What Each Pays For
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Collision Covers the Car You Hit. Comprehensive Covers Everything Else.**

A lot of people bundle these two together in their heads like they're the same thing, but they do very different jobs. Collision coverage pays for damage when your car hits something — another vehicle, a guardrail, a pothole. Comprehensive picks up everything else: hail, a tree branch, a deer, a break-in, a flash flood. If you only have one and not the other, there's a real gap. Worth pulling up your declarations page and confirming both are on there — especially if your car is financed or newer.

Not sure what you're carrying on each vehicle? Your Peachy agent can pull that up fast and walk you through whether the split makes sense for where you are.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/article/what-is-covered-by-collision-and-comprehensive-auto-insurance)
summary: Explains the functional difference between collision and comprehensive auto coverage — collision for impact damage, comprehensive for non-collision events like weather, animals, and theft — and how to confirm both are in place.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for all three newsletters — broad homeowner and auto-owning populations across East Cobb, Perimeter, and Lewisville Lake. Prior auto tips covered road trip coverage, comprehensive coverage confirmation, and deductibles — this tip approaches it from a different angle: defining the functional split between the two coverage types and helping readers identify if they're missing one. Materially different from the June 14 comprehensive tip which focused on what comprehensive pays for during summer; this one is framed around the collision-vs-comprehensive distinction and coverage confirmation. Timeliness is moderate — summer driving and hail season in North Texas and Georgia both make this relevant. Trusted source (iii.org). Not a repeat subject at this angle.
